sentences.text: Well, we arrived there at-- I think it was in August 1944.
And we there till April the 27th, 1945.
To leave?
Well, we-- we worked, of course, there for these months, and we were building that big bunker.
And the Americans came closer.
The British came closer.
And everybody got closer, so they started to liquidate those separate camps.
These camps, by that time, didn't have that much people, that many people, but there were people there.
Because as time went on, they liquidated also some of the camps, and they would bring the people in into those camps where-- where some life would go on.
Like my camp got more people in from other camps that were liquidated.
So we just-- one day, they say they going to send us to Tyrol.
So we thought, well, that's-- that's another place we got to go to.
As they put us on-- on these trains again, and we were driving all night, but we didn't realize that we were going back and forth, back and forth.
Everything was already kind of closed up, because the Wehrmacht, the-- the-- the-- the army had to have the trains.
So they would not give us a chance to go on.
So basically, this is how the whole thing happened that we were able to get out of there.
The same thing.
The trains were no different than the ones that they would always carry us, and that was the cattle trains.
You had to stand, or if it was not that many people you were able to sit or hang sort of like.
There was no way that you were able to really have a comfort.
There was no comfortable train, comfortable transportation.
Just that night, see?
In the morning, they would get us in the morning, and we would, you know, till they get everything-- everybody organized, everything going.
And before you know, it got dark, and it got to day.
And at night, we were sleeping, and a little bit it was raining.
And next morning, we realized that we didn't go very far.
We were in the same woods where we started out.
Oh, just people from the camp, same people.
There were quite a few trains, wagons.
So people only from that camp, lager, you know.
Yes, but it didn't matter, you know?
The people all look alike.
They were all dying.
So it didn't-- there was no-- no connection.
And my uncle was there, and this fellow, Sidney Furman was there.
Yes.

You see, what happened, as we left Auschwitz, something like five or six weeks later [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I got in into one of those camps in-- in-- in Kaufering, this fellow Furman was looking for me.
So we all had-- we all had the stripes on, you must understand, and we all looked alike pretty much.
So he was calling, Haniek, Haniek, Haniek.
He called me Haniek.
So my Uncle Max heard it, and so he went after him until I responded.
And that's how I met my Uncle Max.
We realized that we worked for two weeks together in the same group, and we didn't recognize each other.
We were working in a-- loading cement and stuff like that.
So when you were on-- in the train car, was Uncle Max and Sid in there with you?
Sid, I cannot remember whether he was in the very same car, because somehows he was going in a different group.
But with Uncle Max we ran, because he pushed me at that time.
He kind of said, raus, you have to get away from here.
Yeah, we-- well, it wasn't really-- you see, they let us all out because the Americans came and bombed the train.
The reason they did, because parallel to us was a-- one of those anti-air-- [INAUDIBLE].
The-- the Germans were shooting up.
So they were trying to shoot down to the Germans, and they hit our train.
They thought this was soldiers.
And we lost 185 people there that day that we buried in the-- in the-- in the area there.
Yeah, we have three mass graves.
So this is how-- how it really happened.
I-- and-- and then when they said, come back, you all come back now, it's-- everything is clear, well, I ran away. 200 people ran away.
And out of those 200, I'm sure 180, 175 are alive-- were alive after-- after the war was over.
And then, of course, you know, it happened that we were able to go in.
They were carrying me.
I was-- I was pretty much shot.
I weighed about 96 pounds, and I couldn't walk really anymore.
I was hanging on these two guys.
And I said, please, go, go, go, run, because the Germans were shooting after us.
Leave me here.
They said, no, we're not going to leave you here.
We're going to take you.
And so we walked into the first house, that house that I showed you the picture of, and we asked if we can have some water.
I-- I spoke.
So-- and they saw that it wasn't just water that we need.
And so from here on, they were hiding us in the-- in the hay-- in the hay barns, and they fed us and stuff like this until the Americans came in.
Yes.
The-- the house was-- they were renting this place there, and they were farming it.
It was a family.
They had a-- a son.
Sepal was his name.
And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a-- a kid.
He was, I don't know, maybe 8, 10 years old.
And from there, while we were hiding, they were-- they fed us.
They fed us there.
And these people were nice enough to risk their lives.
And next day, when the Germans came in, they say, you have-- you have here people from camp, and where are they?
They said, we don't have people from camp here.
And they went in into the haystack and started punching and seeing nothing happened.
We were-- we were hidden in there, and that they did not injure us.
And so then they left.
And three days later, the Americans came in.
Do you remember the name of Sepal's parents?
Yes, Trattner.
Yes, I think it's T-R-A-T-T-N-E-R, Joseph Trattner.
Oh, there we were not just laying in the haystack.
When they went away, they took us out, and we, you know, we were able to work around where they had the farm.
They had-- you know, they had all kinds of things, and-- and we helped, you know?
And for about three days, the Americans came in.
Three days later the Americans came in.
Well, that we already-- at night, we already saw that there's a-- an American.
It was the first house.
So I guess when the patrols came in, the American patrols, they were the first ones to walk in into that house.
And then we saw these are Americans.
Yes.
Well, we just looked in.
We didn't-- you know, we were-- [? not ?] that the fear was-- was the Americans talking to the Germans there.
Well, it made us feel very good.
But as I say, we were so dead.
Although I must say, these people gave us all the food we wanted.
They-- they fed us.
I was-- I-- I gained an enormous amount of weight the first few-- few weeks.
And from there on, it was-- you know, we realized there were 20 here, 10 here.
And before we knew, we had something-- we realized we have a lot of refugees around there, people that were in concentration camps.
No, the next day--
You know, when we got out, there were Americans walking around already, you know, in-- in-- in that little town.
Because they-- they were the ones that came in at first.
Petzenhausen.
Yes.
P-E-T-Z-E-N-H-A-U-S-E-N.
Petzenhausen.
Well, they were very, very nice to us, I mean, all the people.
And we found out that it so happened to be that something like 100-and-some-odd houses were there all together living in the-- not houses, but so many people were living in that little town.
And we found out that only one or two of all these people in that town were in the Nazi party.
So somehows we were lucky because, as I said, there were a few hundred-- 200 that run away, but not just all in that town, maybe kept about 50 maybe-- saved.
Yeah, it was in the same train.
It was the same train.
You see, the opportunity that the train was hit by an American bomb, the-- the-- not the train itself only, but the locomotive.
And that did not move the train until they got somebody to fix it and all this.
After the liberation and when the Americans came in?
Well, I-- you know, my father was living at that time in Palestine.
And I had my friend, Furman, and then didn't know.
You know, I was 19 and 1/2 years old.
I was a kid.
I was a nobody.
I didn't get any education.
I did not-- I didn't know.
There's a new world all of a sudden, American soldiers.
And it's not-- you're not going to get killed because you're going to tell them how-- how-- how much you like them or dislike them.
It was a very different life, all of a sudden, open up for us.
So this is something that-- it was very memorable, but very exciting.
But if you-- I couldn't-- I couldn't even today rethink this, you know, how happy a person was.
It was very hard-- it's very hard for me to explain that to you today.
Yes.
After-- after my father knew that I'm alive.
And he knew because the Red Cross came in right away, and they took our names and they-- so I scribbled something and told him.
I had in my mind-- I remembered my father's address in Haifa, Israel.
I mean, now it's Israel, but at that time it was Tel Aviv-- it was Palestine.
And I gave them my father's name, and I told him that I'm alive and that uncle Max is alive.
But the whole family, I don't know if they're alive or they are not alive.
So this is how he knew that I was alive, but he did not-- he tried to write back.
He went to the Red Cross and everything.
Nobody knew where we are.
And so in-- in October or so, when I was already in Bremen, I met an American soldier, and I asked him if he could-- because the-- the mail didn't trans-- didn't go from one country to the other.
It was very difficult for anybody to write.
I could not write a letter to Palestine.
So he wrote to his parents in London.
He was living in London, worked there, being in the American Army.
He was in the CIC, counterintelligence.
And he send that letter to his father, and his father send the letter to Palestine, and then my father would write to his father.
And that's how I would then know that my father is alive.
So that's how we got actually together again.
Did you tell your father about the fate of your mother and sister?
Yes, yes, immediately.
I mean, we knew that-- this was October, November, you know, and it's-- it was too long gone that-- that they are alive.
I don't think I really considered that ever, really, to go back to Poland.
If anything, maybe just to go back and get a look.
But other than that, no.
Yes.
I went just for a very short while, like a few days, two or three days.
But I never-- I said I was enough in camps.
I'm not going to go back into a camp, and I didn't.
And this is the time that we took the trip to go up north, and we went to Bremen.
Well, actually we did decide offhand to go to Bremen.
We-- we-- we were able to get tickets to go to Palestine at the time.
The Haganah, the-- the Jewish agencies and everything, organized people, young people, to come there and fight for-- for Israel.
And so we finally, finally got a-- a trip.
So as we were on the bus-- not the bus, the truck, a guy sits there.
Ah, he recognized my friend Sid [?
Furman. ?] He said, what are you doing here?
He said, what do you mean, the same thing you are doing here.
He said, don't you know your sister is alive?
That's Sid's.
So it-- and she was in-- in Bergen-Belsen.
And so-- so I said to him, listen, let's jump off this bus, truck, and let's go to Bergen-Belsen first.
It took us seven days to get up there because everything was bombed out.
There was no train.
There was no things.
Some of the farmers gave us a ride and some of the-- whatever-- whichever way we could.
And we got into Hanover, which is not far away from Bergen-Belsen, and we found out that his sister died four weeks after the war.
So that, of course, was the end of that trip.
And then as we were there to-- to that-- that evening, as we came in there there was a party, and we met this Polish officer.
And he said, hey, you guys, you don't want to stay here.
Let's go.
We want to go to England.
Because over there the-- they're waiting to take us to Palestine.
The only thing we have to go is go to Hamburg, and from Hamburg we go to England.
From England, we go to Palestine.
It wasn't true.
He just was dreaming about up, and that-- that was it.
So we went by train to go to Hamburg.
We did believe him at the time.
We thought, well, what are we going to do here in Hanover?
We have to run someplace.
So as we got on the train, the train wouldn't go to Hanover that-- to-- to Hamburg that day.
So it stopped in Bremen.
So we have to-- they say, well, this train is not going to go any further because the-- the-- the rails are not in good shape.
We should come back tomorrow.

So tomorrow we-- we went and that's-- that evening I met a lady in the street with a blue and white little swastika here she had on the little-- and-- and so I asked her what-- what do Jewish people do when they come here?
She says, I don't know.
I just came back.
My father came back.
I'm together with my father in one room.
But we don't have any room for you guys, for those three people.
So she says, why don't you go-- there's an American soldier.
He is an officer, and he tries to help as much as he can whoever passes by here.
His name is Joe Feiner.
And so we went there, and he said, listen, there's no trains waiting for you.
There's no ships waiting for you.
Nobody's going to take you to Palestine.
You're going to stay here, and I'm going to see to it that you're going to stay overnight here, and he did.
And this is how we got stuck in Bremen.
And this Joe Feiner became my partner here in Tulsa, Oklahoma, and we started Fabricut together.
No, not in Germany.
I never did.
I never did.
Maybe it was hidden.
Maybe-- I don't know what they did when I didn't talk with them, but when I spoke with them and stuff.
I guess, you know, with the Germans, it's like this.
They-- some of them knew what was going on, some of them.
Maybe the majority knew about it.
But nobody could think that such a thing is going to happen, nobody, nobody, but nobody.
Today when I think about it, there isn't a German around that would think that what happened, at the time, that they would do such a thing.
So I think this too, in a way, came as a shock to a lot of Germans.
In Bremen, I came into Bremen, and I met-- a few days later, I met my good wife.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got-- through my friend he got us an apartment.
And there was Furman and myself and-- and that Polack.
And then he disappeared and took our stuff, [LAUGHS] and we didn't have anything to wear.
He was an officer.
But whatever it was, he-- he-- we just didn't see him again.
And we-- my friend, Sid [?
Furman, ?] he was a tailor, so he got a job with the army, with the American Army, right away.
And I didn't have any trade.
I didn't learn anything.
I was dumb.
I was, in a way, very inexperienced in life.
So you know, there was a lot of-- little black market going on.
People bought cigarettes and sold cigarettes.
And-- and it was-- bought dollars and sold dollars.
It was a little bit going on that you could make a living out of it, and that's what I did.
I did not have a steady job to go in the morning and go and work.
You mentioned meeting your wife-to-be.
What was her name?
Ursula.
Weller, W-E-L-L-E-R.
We met in the Jewish-- I went to the Jewish community the next day, or a few days later.
And I had my-- you had to fill out where you came from and all this and, you know, that-- because people were looking for people.
And it was registered into a book that people could find one another.
So-- and so she filled out all these-- like, she was a secretary working in the Jewish community.
And I figured, well, if she can type so well, maybe it would be a good idea to marry her.
So that's what I did, [LAUGHS] after a while.
No, it wasn't.
But it was-- we-- we engaged and married about six months later, a year later.
Oh, very festive, very festive.
Yeah, by that time, I was already in business.
I had a business in Bremen.
I-- I started out a cabaret business and a restaurant business.
It was a-- the top-notch business in Bremen at that time.
This was 1945, '46.
I opened it in January '46.
And I was doing very well, extremely well.
It was a very, very nice place.
We could accommodate, summertime, maybe 1,000 people, because it was in a park, you know, very famous park.
And that's how I-- [INAUDIBLE] I kept-- I had it for two years.
And that's where the marriage ceremony was, in-- in that place, because it was, as I said, we had a band of 11 people, and we had everything.
We had a variety show and-- and so forth.
It was-- it was a very nice, very festive wedding we had.
Yes, it was.
We went to a-- a Jewish synagogue.
It was set up-- a temporary thing.
And we had-- we had a-- an American chaplain married us.
And it was quite-- quite an-- an occasion in Bremen for our wedding.
It was a very, very, important occasion in Bremen.
Yes.
That was a big question for myself, too, at the time.
I didn't know whether I should or not.
But my wife Ursula didn't want to stay in Germany.
And she-- so I guess she put a little pressure on me that we should leave.
And I left this business with a good manager.
He was a very good manager.
And we-- we left it there.
We left the business, and-- and we came to the United States.
It was very-- actually, I did not have a very complicated immigration problem.
Why?
I don't know.
We were in Bremen.
Bremen didn't have that many refugees as, let's say, Munich or other cities had.
So-- and explaining to the counselor that, in a way, I-- I don't-- I want to make a living.
I want to live in a free country.
I want to have all the good things that are in a-- in a free country like America.
So I guess he bought it, in a way.
And of course, my wife, being born in Germany, that [INAUDIBLE] was better than the Polish [INAUDIBLE].
I had the Polish boarder.
She had the German boarder.
So they took that into consideration, and that helped us go.
By boat.
We came with the And the was one of the troop ships that brought the troops to-- to Europe and back.
It was an American ship.
And we came-- with that ship we came to America.
Yes, yeah, about 900 or 600.
And we had a tough trip.
It was in January, very, very rough trip.
And that's how we arrived in America on-- in-- in January 1947, the 16th of January.
New York.
Yeah, we came into New York.
It was very cold.
And it was very interesting and all these big buildings.
And for us, it was fantastic.
No.
But my wife, she was-- Ursula was a little more-- she was more intelligent.
She had more education.
She-- she knew some English.
So yeah, it was better for her than for me.
For me, it was very hard.
In New York?
Yes, 153rd Street in Manhattan.
Yes.
There was the-- the-- a Jewish organization.
You see, all the refugees, as they come to-- came to the United States, the churches, the synagogues, the-- all kinds of organizations would help.
Me, being Jewish, it was, of course, the-- the-- they took care of me, the-- for new Americans, and they helped us out with the first, I think, $50.
We got $200 or whatever.
And that money, with that money we were able to buy the first apartment.
Well, we are not religious people.
But we are-- we-- we believe in being Jewish.
But we don't believe necessarily about, you know, all the religious part of the Jewish people.
So we went to synagogue once in a while, yes.
I went to a-- they had set up in New York-- there were the schools set up for the refugees.
And it was a-- a school that I went to, I think, 20 times and 20 lessons.
And that's how I learned.
And above all, I learned is in the movies, because you learn a lot because there were two features, and it was like a three-hour movie deal.
And we went at least once a week.
Yes.
After applying to become a-- a US citizen.
After five years, you become a citizen, and that's what we did.
To start off with, I became a very good presser.
And I worked on-- in-- in that-- with that company four and a half years.
And then after this, I went to work for my friend, Joe Feiner, that I met in Bremen, and I went to Huntington.
